# Transactional Net Promoter Score®: Which is the best way to collect data?

![customer loyalty](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20900%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![customer loyalty](https://www.genroe.com/wp-content/uploads/bigstock-Conceptual-The-Customer-Respon-322454434.jpg)

# Transactional Net Promoter Score®: Which is the best way to collect data?

![Picture of Adam Ramshaw](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Picture of Adam Ramshaw](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/ec402970c86690fc74ae10649f61e248b9f52c51afc748f457b3cc4b88bb84ad?s=300&d=mm&r=g)

With the expanding use of [Transactional Net Promoter Score](https://www.genroe.com/net-promoter-score "transactional net promoter score") by a range of organisations, there is a growing need to [collect large volumes of survey responses](https://www.genroe.com/net-promoter-score/tools) with a smaller amount of feedback in each survey.

This is because the standard Transactional NPS® (TNPS) survey is just two questions long:

## What is Transactional Net Promoter Score (TNPS)?

Transactional Net Promoter Score is the process of surveying your customers after an interaction, or transaction, with your organisation. This generates very actionable data on how to improve the touch-points in the customer journey and is a very effective way of improving NPS.

Typically this will be at one or more of your customer touch points and can include:

## How to collect Transactional Net Promoter Score data

There are currently four main approaches used to survey customers for TNPS:

### **1. Outbound telephone calls**:

Using call centre agents to call each (or a selected set of) customer(s) and perform the survey.

### **2. Inbound, agent diverted calls**:

At the end of a call the customer is asked if they mind answering a couple of questions on the transaction.  If they agree they are immediately transferred to a contact centre agent to take their feedback.

### **3. Inbound IVR**:

Inbound callers are directed to an IVR based survey engine at the end of the call.  The caller answers using the key pad and the system records their qualitative comments.

### **4. Email/Web surveys**:

A survey system sends an email to each customer with a link to a web based survey.  The respondent then clicks on the link and fills out the survey on line.

## The Best Transactional NPS Data Collection Approach

Each has their advantages but which is the best?

Before we can answer that we need to look at the key requirements for a successful Transactional NPS survey process:

### 1. **Timely**:

An effective TNPS process is one where the customer is contacted very soon after the service or transactional interaction.  This ensures that the interaction is fresh in the respondent’s mind.

### 2. **Efficiently gather qualitative feedback**:

The second of the two standard TNPS questions is qualitative in nature.  It’s role is key as it helps the organisation to understand how to make changes to improve their business.

### 3. **Drive closed loop action**:

More so than relationship NPS, transactional NPS is focused on closing the loop to immediate action.  The cycle time for this process should be measured in hours not days or weeks.

### 4. **Support live data analysis**:

As TNPS is an on-going survey process the organisation must have access to an on-going data analysis approach that allows it to understand and action the data received in a timely manner.

### 5. **Deliver high volumes**:

TNPS is typified by a high volume of customer contacts each and every day.  It is this high volume of customer contact that provides a constant stream of feedback to all employees.

So how does our list of requirements mesh with the different survey approaches?

## 1. Outbound telephone calls

While they can be timely, the ability to connect with a high volume of customers comes at a high cost to the organisation.  To efficiently contact enough customers also requires relatively advanced contact centre technology and skills.

However, the agent is able to ask customers about their experience and gather a good volume and quality of qualitative feedback.

But, having collected the data it still requires additional systems to drive the closed loop action and data analysis.

Outbound calls also suffer from a unique issue in that that respondents may not feel comfortable providing honest feedback to another person from that company, particularly negative feedback about a specific person.

## 2. Inbound, agent diverted calls

In this approach the timeliness of the process is assured as the customer is connected immediately after the transaction has been performed.    However, of course, there must be an operator available to take the call and the relative cost is high versus the fully automated approaches below.

Forwarding a customer to a holding queue will drive down the score for the survey and reduce the conversion rate.  It is also a poor customer experience in it’s own right.

Also, using this method eliminates the ability to deal with a range of contact or transaction channels in an integrated manner.  It only really works for contact centre transactions.

The ability to “game” this approach is also high with agents able to push happy customers to do the survey, or more often, to neglect to inform unhappy customers about the survey.  This gives the operator the ability to affect their personal NPS.

Having collected the data it still requires additional systems to drive the closed loop action and data analysis.

## **3. Inbound IVR**

The ability to be timely is high as the diversion to the IVR is immediate and there is no need to worry about an agent being available.

One key drawback is that the qualitative data is collected as recorded audio information.  In order to turn that data into action it must first be listened to or transcribed.  Both of these data collection approaches are expensive and time consuming, reducing the speed of response and increasing overall cost.

This technique also suffers from the ability of operators to game the system, similar to that noted above.

Anecdotally the survey completion rate is also lower than average as customers don’t want to wait on the phone more than necessary.

Having collected the data it still requires additional systems to drive the closed loop action and data analysis.

For a more detailed evaluation of Inbound IVR see this post: [Post Call IVR Surveys: Popular But Not That Useful](https://www.genroe.com/blog/post-call-ivr-surveys-popular-but-not-that-useful/8447 "Post Call IVR Surveys: Popular But Not That Useful")

## 4. Email/Web surveys

Using [web surveys for transactional Net Promoter Score](https://www.genroe.com/net-promoter-score/software "net promoter score surveys") delivers on all of the key requirement.

The survey can be sent out the next day, or even sooner if required.  Customers are familiar with the email survey approach and so response rates are generally high (20-40%).

When gathering qualitative feedback, the customer can tag their comment with the area of the business to which it relates.  This greatly speeds up the analysis process and makes the whole approach much more action oriented.

It can also be rapidly scaled to very large volumes with little extra cost.

So long as the web survey engine has the right tools to rapidly analyse and report on the data in **Net Promoter Score terms** this can be a very effective approach.

Lastly, this approach can be used across all the channels in an organisation and is not limited to just contact centre transactions.

# Transactional Data Explained: Definitions, Examples, and Use Cases

![starrocks_slack](https://celerdata.com/hs-fs/hubfs/celerdata_2023/slack.png?width=116&name=slack.png)

Join StarRocks Community on Slack

## What Is Transactional Data

### 

### Definition and Characteristics

Transactional data refers to the information captured during transactions. This data records every detail of an event, providing a comprehensive view of each transaction. You will find that transactional data includes timestamps, user IDs, and transaction types. It serves as a digital footprint of business activities.

#### Key Features of Transactional Data

Transactional data possesses several key features. First, it is dynamic. New transactions constantly update the data, reflecting real-time changes. Second, it is detailed. Each transaction records specific information, such as the time, location, and payment method. Third, transactional data is structured. It follows a consistent format, making it easy to analyze and interpret.

#### Examples of Transactional Data

You encounter transactional data in various forms. For instance, when you purchase a product online, the system records the transaction details. These include the item purchased, the price, and the payment method. Similarly, when you withdraw money from an ATM, the bank captures the transaction data, noting the amount and time of withdrawal. These examples illustrate how transactional data permeates everyday activities.

### Importance in Business

Transactional data plays a crucial role in business operations. It provides insights that help companies make informed decisions and improve customer experiences.

#### Role in Decision Making

Businesses rely on transactional data for decision-making. By analyzing this data, you can identify trends and patterns. For example, a retailer might notice increased sales of a particular product during a specific season. This insight allows the retailer to adjust inventory levels accordingly. Transactional data empowers you to make strategic decisions based on factual evidence.

#### Impact on Customer Experience

Transactional data significantly impacts customer experience. By understanding customer behavior, you can tailor services to meet their needs. For instance, analyzing purchase history helps businesses offer personalized recommendations. This enhances customer satisfaction and fosters loyalty. Transactional data enables you to create a more engaging and fulfilling customer journey.

## 

## Types of Transactional Data

Understanding the types of transactional data helps you grasp how businesses operate. This data captures both financial and non-financial activities, providing a comprehensive view of operations.

### Financial Transactions

Financial transactions form the backbone of business operations. They include sales and purchases, as well as banking activities.

#### Sales and Purchases

Sales data is crucial for any business. It records every transaction involving the exchange of goods or services for money. You can find this data in a sales table, which details items sold, prices, and payment methods. E-commerce platforms rely heavily on this data to track online purchases. By analyzing sales data, you can identify trends and adjust strategies accordingly.

Purchases, on the other hand, involve acquiring goods or services. This data helps businesses manage inventory and budget effectively. Access to accurate purchase data ensures that you maintain optimal stock levels and avoid overbuying.

#### Banking Transactions

Banking transactions include deposits, withdrawals, and transfers. This data is essential for financial institutions to manage accounts and ensure security. Protected access to banking data safeguards sensitive information. You can monitor your financial activities and make informed decisions based on this data.

### Non-Financial Transactions

Non-financial transactions capture interactions and movements that don't involve money directly. They provide insights into customer behavior and inventory management.

#### Customer Interactions

Customer interactions generate valuable data. This includes feedback, inquiries, and service requests. By collecting transactional data from these interactions, you can enhance customer experiences. Understanding customer data allows you to tailor services and improve satisfaction. E-commerce platforms use this data to personalize recommendations and foster loyalty.

#### Inventory Movements

Inventory movements track the flow of goods within a business. This data helps you manage stock levels and prevent shortages. Access to real-time inventory data ensures that you can respond quickly to demand changes. By analyzing this data, you can optimize supply chain operations and reduce costs.

## 

## How Transactional Data Is Collected

Understanding how data is collected helps you appreciate its role in business operations. Various methods capture transactional data, each offering unique insights.

### Data Collection Methods

#### 

#### Point of Sale Systems

Point of Sale (POS) systems play a crucial role in collecting data. When you make a purchase at a store, the POS system records details like the item, price, and payment method. This data helps businesses track sales and manage inventory. By analyzing POS data, you can identify popular products and adjust stock levels accordingly.

#### Online Transactions

Online transactions generate a wealth of data. Every time you buy something online, the system captures information such as the product, payment details, and delivery address. This data provides insights into customer preferences and buying habits. E-commerce platforms use this data to personalize your shopping experience and improve service offerings.

### Challenges in Data Collection

Collecting data presents challenges that you must address to ensure accuracy and privacy.

#### Data Accuracy

Ensuring data accuracy is vital. Inaccurate data can lead to poor decision-making. You need to implement checks and validations to maintain data integrity. Regular audits and updates help keep the data reliable. Accurate data empowers you to make informed business decisions.

#### Privacy Concerns

Privacy concerns are paramount when collecting data. You must protect customer information to maintain trust. Implementing robust security measures safeguards sensitive data. Transparency about data usage builds confidence with customers. By prioritizing privacy, you ensure compliance with regulations and foster customer loyalty.

## 

## How Is Transactional Data Managed

Effectively managing transactional data is essential for any business. You need to understand how data storage solutions and management techniques work to ensure data integrity and accessibility.

### Data Storage Solutions

#### 

#### Databases

Databases play a crucial role in storing transactional data. You often use relational databases like SQL Server or MySQL. These systems organize data into tables, making it easy to retrieve and analyze. Relational databases ensure data consistency and reliability. They follow the ACID properties, which guarantee that transactions are processed accurately. By using a relational database, you can efficiently manage large volumes of data.

#### Cloud Storage

Cloud storage offers flexibility and scalability for data management. You can store data remotely, allowing access from anywhere with an internet connection. This solution supports businesses that need to scale their operations quickly. Cloud storage providers offer robust security measures to protect your data. By leveraging cloud storage, you can reduce infrastructure costs and improve data accessibility.

### Data Management Techniques

#### 

#### Data Cleansing

[Data cleansing](/glossary/data-cleansing) is vital for maintaining data quality. You need to identify and correct errors in your data. This process involves removing duplicates and correcting inaccuracies. Clean data ensures that your analyses are accurate and reliable. By regularly cleansing your data, you enhance decision-making and operational efficiency.

#### Data Integration

Data integration combines data from different sources into a unified view. You often integrate transactional data with master data and reference data. This integration provides a comprehensive understanding of business operations. By merging data, you can uncover insights that drive strategic decisions. Effective data integration enhances your ability to respond to market changes and customer needs.

## 

## Applications of Transactional Data

Transactional data plays a vital role in enhancing business operations. By leveraging this data, you can gain insights that drive growth and improve customer experiences.

### Business Intelligence

Transactional data serves as the backbone of business intelligence. It helps you make informed decisions by providing a clear picture of your operations.

#### Analytics and Reporting

Analytics and reporting are essential for understanding your business landscape. By analyzing sales data, you can identify trends and patterns. This analysis allows you to adjust strategies and optimize performance. For instance, tracking sales figures helps you understand which products are performing well. You can then focus on these areas to boost revenue.

#### Predictive Modeling

Predictive modeling uses historical data to forecast future outcomes. By examining past sales, you can predict future demand. This insight helps you manage inventory and plan marketing campaigns. Predictive modeling empowers you to stay ahead of market trends and make proactive decisions.

### Enhancing Customer Relationships

Building strong customer relationships is crucial for any organization. Transactional data provides the insights needed to personalize interactions and foster loyalty.

#### Personalization

Personalization enhances the customer experience by tailoring services to individual needs. By analyzing purchase history, you can offer personalized recommendations. This approach makes customers feel valued and understood. Personalized experiences lead to increased satisfaction and repeat business.

#### Customer Retention Strategies

Retaining customers is more cost-effective than acquiring new ones. Transactional data helps you develop effective retention strategies. By understanding buying behavior, you can create targeted promotions and loyalty programs. These initiatives encourage repeat purchases and strengthen customer bonds.

## 

## Future Trends in Transactional Data

As you explore the future of transactional data, you will encounter exciting developments. Emerging technologies and evolving data regulations are shaping how businesses manage and utilize this data. Understanding these trends will help you stay ahead in the ever-changing digital landscape.

### Emerging Technologies

Emerging technologies are revolutionizing the way you handle transactional data. They offer new possibilities for enhancing data processing and security.

#### AI and Machine Learning

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) are transforming data analysis. These technologies enable you to process large volumes of transactional data quickly. AI algorithms can identify patterns and trends that might be missed by human analysis. By leveraging AI and ML, you can make more accurate predictions and informed decisions. These tools enhance your ability to understand customer behavior and optimize business strategies.

#### Blockchain

Blockchain technology is gaining traction in transactional data management. It provides a secure and transparent way to record transactions. Each transaction is stored in a block, which is linked to the previous one, forming a chain. This structure ensures data integrity and prevents unauthorized alterations. Blockchain's decentralized nature enhances security, making it ideal for sensitive data. By adopting blockchain, you can improve trust and transparency in your transactions. This technology is particularly useful in industries like finance and supply chain management.

### Evolving Data Regulations

Data regulations are evolving to address privacy and security concerns. Staying informed about these changes is crucial for compliance and effective data management.

#### GDPR and CCPA

The [General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)](/glossary/general-data-protection-regulation-gdpr) and the [California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)](/glossary/california-consumer-privacy-act-ccpa) are two significant data protection laws. They aim to safeguard personal information and give individuals more control over their data. As a business, you must comply with these regulations to avoid penalties. This involves implementing measures to protect customer data and ensuring transparency in data usage. Understanding GDPR and CCPA requirements helps you build trust with your customers and maintain a positive reputation.

#### Impact on Data Management

Evolving data regulations impact how you manage transactional data. You need to adopt practices that prioritize data privacy and security. This includes using encryption and access controls to protect sensitive information. Regular audits and updates ensure compliance with changing regulations. By focusing on data management, you can enhance customer trust and reduce the risk of data breaches. Staying informed about regulatory changes allows you to adapt quickly and maintain compliance.

## 

## Conclusion

Transactional data holds immense value for your business. It captures every detail of transactions, offering insights into customer behavior and market trends. By understanding this data, you can make informed decisions and enhance customer experiences.

Leverage transactional data to drive growth. Use it to optimize operations, personalize customer interactions, and stay ahead of market trends. Embrace the power of data to transform your business and achieve success in today's competitive landscape.

# What is transactional data and how can you leverage it?

August 25, 2022

![What is transactional data and how can you leverage it](https://www.lytics.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/article-What-is-transactional-data-and-how-can-you-leverage-it_.jpg)

There are many sources of information available online that are useful for analyzing customers and their behaviors. Whether they’ve made a purchase or not, online customers and site visitors can provide data and insights to your brand that can help make your marketing more effective and allow your advertising and digital marketing dollars to go further.

One example of readily available data is transactional data. Let’s explore what transactional data is and how it can be used to spur growth.

## What is transactional data?

Transactional data is any information that’s gathered as the result of a transaction. While it is easy to assume that the transaction must involve a purchase, the fact is that online, a transaction is merely defined as an “event.” These events simply mean that information is being shared between the site visitor’s web browser and your site or organization.

The key feature that sets transactional data apart from other types of collected data is that it provides a date and time. This temporal detail helps to provide context and gives it an objective piece of data to use as a pivot point when [analyzing customer](https://www.lytics.com/blog/customer-analytics-why-and-how-to-track-them/) behaviors.

## How to leverage transactional data

When analyzed properly, transactional data becomes hugely important. It provides [context to everyday transactions](https://wcapra.com/leveraging-transaction-data-capraplus-your-source-for-insight/) and helps organizations better understand what their customers are actually doing online. Transactional data allows you to follow the clicks and trace how your customers behave before and after a purchase. It can even help identify reasons that customers are dropping off the site before making a purchase or abandoning their shopping carts.

## Transactional data examples

There are many different ways that transactional data can be used to help grow your business. Here are two specific use cases that might seem familiar:

### 1. Gaining insight from pre- and post-purchase customer data

One primary example of transactional data involves recording the time, date, and type of purchase a customer makes on your site. While the type of product the customer purchased, as well as how much they spent, will be captured by your organization’s point-of-sale program, there is other related data that’s considered transactional.

Information regarding the customer’s pre-purchase behavior is also gathered. Whether or not they compared products before making a final selection or if they chose to use a promo code is all information that is gathered as part of the transaction.

With this information, you can answer questions such as:

* How many times did they browse the product that they ended up purchasing?
* Did they look at any similar products or read reviews before purchasing?
* If a customer browsed the product previously but chose to purchase it today, why is that? (The assumption could be made that it’s a result of the promo code offer that was sent.)

Each of these questions and answers leads to a better understanding of your customers and their habits. While it may not be feasible to run a promotion every day, you can aggregate the data regarding promo code usage, for example, and draw additional conclusions. Perhaps only promo codes that offer a savings of 15% or more spur an actual purchase. This information can further inform your marketing team of what types of offers are the most effective.

### 2. Removing sales obstacles

In a very real way, transactional data can remove impediments from customers making their final purchase. With the data that’s gathered, you’ll be able to note if certain payment types failed or if a customer got all the way to the checkout screen and then stopped the transaction because they couldn’t find the payment method they wanted.

This information makes it easier for you to troubleshoot technical issues with any of your financial partners and can help you determine if additional payment options are needed. It can also help streamline your website. The goal should be for a seamless experience from the moment a guest hits checkout to when they hit place order. The more clicks, forms, fields, and error messages they get along the way, the more opportunities they have to walk away. That means you lose a sale and they get frustrated. With transactional data, you can proactively address many of these issues.

## Transactional data is too big to go alone

As with a lot of collected data, the biggest obstacle most businesses face is figuring out how to make sense of it. So much data is collected every second of every day, that finding a path through the chaos can be nearly impossible.

This is true 10 times over in regards to transactional data. With so many different types of transactional data and just as many ways to apply it, most brands don’t have the bandwidth to do the hard work of analyzing the data and identifying behaviors. That’s why firms like Lytics are so important. With their experience in data modeling, Lytics can easily break down your data, identify your most important target audiences, segment them accordingly, and reach more of the customers you want faster.

[![](https://www.lytics.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/inline-banner-how-to-pivot.png)](/resources/whitepapers/how-to-pivot-to-a-first-party-data-marketing-strategy/)

There are many sources of information available online that are useful for analyzing customers and their behaviors. Whether they’ve made a purchase or not, online customers and site visitors can provide data and insights to your brand that can help make your marketing more effective and allow your advertising and digital marketing dollars to go further.

One example of readily available data is transactional data. Let’s explore what transactional data is and how it can be used to spur growth.

## What is transactional data?

Transactional data is any information that’s gathered as the result of a transaction. While it is easy to assume that the transaction must involve a purchase, the fact is that online, a transaction is merely defined as an “event.” These events simply mean that information is being shared between the site visitor’s web browser and your site or organization.

The key feature that sets transactional data apart from other types of collected data is that it provides a date and time. This temporal detail helps to provide context and gives it an objective piece of data to use as a pivot point when [analyzing customer](https://www.lytics.com/blog/customer-analytics-why-and-how-to-track-them/) behaviors.

## How to leverage transactional data

When analyzed properly, transactional data becomes hugely important. It provides [context to everyday transactions](https://wcapra.com/leveraging-transaction-data-capraplus-your-source-for-insight/) and helps organizations better understand what their customers are actually doing online. Transactional data allows you to follow the clicks and trace how your customers behave before and after a purchase. It can even help identify reasons that customers are dropping off the site before making a purchase or abandoning their shopping carts.

## Transactional data examples

There are many different ways that transactional data can be used to help grow your business. Here are two specific use cases that might seem familiar:

### 1. Gaining insight from pre- and post-purchase customer data

One primary example of transactional data involves recording the time, date, and type of purchase a customer makes on your site. While the type of product the customer purchased, as well as how much they spent, will be captured by your organization’s point-of-sale program, there is other related data that’s considered transactional.

Information regarding the customer’s pre-purchase behavior is also gathered. Whether or not they compared products before making a final selection or if they chose to use a promo code is all information that is gathered as part of the transaction.

With this information, you can answer questions such as:

Each of these questions and answers leads to a better understanding of your customers and their habits. While it may not be feasible to run a promotion every day, you can aggregate the data regarding promo code usage, for example, and draw additional conclusions. Perhaps only promo codes that offer a savings of 15% or more spur an actual purchase. This information can further inform your marketing team of what types of offers are the most effective.

### 2. Removing sales obstacles

In a very real way, transactional data can remove impediments from customers making their final purchase. With the data that’s gathered, you’ll be able to note if certain payment types failed or if a customer got all the way to the checkout screen and then stopped the transaction because they couldn’t find the payment method they wanted.

This information makes it easier for you to troubleshoot technical issues with any of your financial partners and can help you determine if additional payment options are needed. It can also help streamline your website. The goal should be for a seamless experience from the moment a guest hits checkout to when they hit place order. The more clicks, forms, fields, and error messages they get along the way, the more opportunities they have to walk away. That means you lose a sale and they get frustrated. With transactional data, you can proactively address many of these issues.

## Transactional data is too big to go alone

As with a lot of collected data, the biggest obstacle most businesses face is figuring out how to make sense of it. So much data is collected every second of every day, that finding a path through the chaos can be nearly impossible.

This is true 10 times over in regards to transactional data. With so many different types of transactional data and just as many ways to apply it, most brands don’t have the bandwidth to do the hard work of analyzing the data and identifying behaviors. That’s why firms like Lytics are so important. With their experience in data modeling, Lytics can easily break down your data, identify your most important target audiences, segment them accordingly, and reach more of the customers you want faster.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Transactional records capture detailed information from sales and customer interactions, providing real-time insights into purchasing behavior and helping optimize pricing strategies.
Surveys collect qualitative insights directly from customers, allowing businesses to gauge satisfaction and perceptions, which informs pricing and service adjustments.
Loyalty programs gather data on repeat customers, enabling businesses to analyze purchasing patterns and tailor pricing strategies to enhance customer retention.
Market analysis tools aggregate external data, providing competitive insights and market trends that inform pricing strategies and positioning in the marketplace.
Web scraping techniques harvest competitor pricing and promotional strategies, allowing businesses to adapt their pricing in response to market dynamics and competitive pressures.
